talk_title: Towards New Cancer Medicines with Degraders of Chromatin
MOA_summary: Danette presented a framework that quantifies degrader kinetics by measuring degradation rate versus Dmax, defining an inflection point (the "rate cliff") that predicts complete target loss across multiple chromatin regulators; fast degraders with high rate efficiency translate to robust in vivo pharmacodynamics independent of DC50.
key_findings: A universal rate‑versus‑Dmax relationship was observed across diverse degraders (BRD9, EP300, CBP) regardless of target or cell line.
The rate inflection point (0.7–1.8) predicts ≥85–90% Dmax; compounds below this "cliff" show target recovery.
AUC of rate‑versus‑concentration curves distinguishes compounds with identical DC50 but up to five‑fold differences in maximal degradation rate.
Faster degraders exhibit higher ubiquitination levels and form more stable ternary complexes with slower koff rates measured by SPR.
In vivo PK/PD mapping of CBP degraders demonstrates that dosing at Cmax and trough samples the maximal degradation rate, leading to robust target loss in mouse models.

talk_title: Designing Effective Degrader Therapeutics: What Early Clinical Experience Has Taught Us

targets.dependency_context: relapsed CLL with BTK resistance mutations

biomarkers.name: BTK resistance mutations
biomarkers.finding: Degrader retained activity against all tested BTK resistance mutations, unlike inhibitors which showed gaps in coverage

models.name: Phase 1 A/B BTK degrader trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Relapsed CLL patients, many with prior BTK inhibitor exposure, BTK resistance mutations, subset with CNS disease

clinical_data.population: heavily pretreated CLL, including BTK‑inhibitor failures

clinical_data.value: No increase in AE rate at 600 mg vs all doses

MOA_summary: Bexipridil is a BTK degrader that induces proteasomal removal of BTK, achieving sub‑stoichiometric target clearance; this eliminates residual kinase activity and overcomes a broad spectrum of BTK resistance mutations, providing deeper and more durable responses than BTK inhibitors.
key_findings: 83% overall response rate in a heavily pre‑treated CLL population, including patients who progressed on the approved BTK inhibitor pirtobrutinib.
Median duration of response reached 20 months, exceeding the ~12‑month DOR reported for the best current inhibitor.
Degrader retained activity against all known BTK resistance mutations, whereas inhibitors showed gaps in coverage.
Higher dose (600 mg) improved progression‑free survival without increasing adverse‑event frequency, indicating a wide therapeutic index.
Comprehensive proteomics showed BTK as one of the top‑down‑regulated proteins with minimal off‑target degradation.
No QT prolongation observed in healthy volunteers or patients, differentiating this degrader from earlier molecules.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: A novel BTK A428D mutation abolishes binding of both inhibitors and degraders, representing a potential resistance mechanism.
open_questions: What therapeutic strategies can overcome the emergent A428D BTK mutation that blocks degrader binding?

talk_title: First‑in‑Class Dual AR/ARV7 Molecular Glue Degraders

targets.dependency_context: CRPC cells expressing AR splice variant V7

biomarkers.name: ARV7 expression
biomarkers.finding: High ARV7 levels predict resistance to enzalutamide; compound 15 degrades ARV7 and restores sensitivity

models.name: LNCaP 95
models.genotype_or_key_features: AR ligand‑binding mutation + ARV7 expression, enzalutamide‑resistant

models.name: 22RV1
models.genotype_or_key_features: Endogenous ARV7 and full‑length AR, CRPC xenograft source

models.name: not specified
models.genotype_or_key_features: patient‑derived CLL samples used for proteomics (for context only)

MOA_summary: Compounds 15 and 64 act as molecular glues that bind the N‑terminal domain of AR/ARV7 and recruit a Cullin‑RING ligase complex (DDB1‑containing) to induce proteasomal degradation of both isoforms, thereby suppressing AR‑driven transcription and overcoming enzalutamide resistance.
key_findings: High‑throughput screen of 170,000 compounds identified 13 hits; lead chemotype 7907 degraded both AR and ARV7 via proteasome.
Medicinal chemistry optimization yielded analogs 15 and 64 with sub‑micromolar EC50s and rapid (≤4 h) degradation of both proteins.
Compound 15 suppressed AR‑driven transcription in 22RV1 cells, outperforming enzalutamide and ARV110 in RNA‑seq AR‑score analysis.
In vivo, oral dosing of compounds 15 and 64 markedly inhibited growth of ARV7‑positive 22RV1 xenografts, achieving greater tumor regression than enzalutamide or ARV110.
Mechanistic studies mapped activity to the AR N‑terminal TAL1 and AF1 subdomains; degradation required Cullin‑RING ligase activity (blocked by MLN4924) and was partially reversed by DDB1 knockout.
Global proteomics showed AR and ARV7 among the top‑down‑regulated proteins with minimal off‑target effects; compounds were well tolerated with no weight loss.
open_questions: What is the precise E3 ligase complex (beyond DDB1) mediating degradation?
Can off‑target degradation be further minimized in primary prostate cells?
